speaker: interviewer
text: March the 21st, 1997. Interview of Miriam Moskowitz, tape number six. You arrived in America. What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: On November 21, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in New York, and we were met my husband's cousin, waiting for us by the boat. We were taken to Passaic, New Jersey. We arrived in Passaic by my husband's uncle. And my husband's four aunts, his mother's sisters came to see us. We stayed in Passaic a few days. My husband was very torn, because he was liberated, not even had a picture of his family. But he cried terribly when he was between his family over here. We stayed in Passaic four days. After four days, Sol's uncle's daughter lived in Brooklyn and she found out that I came here as a nurse, took us to Brooklyn, and she gave me a job as a nurse. I started to work in a hospital, and my husband couldn't find work. He came here as electric technician, and he also had a diploma from ORT as a furrier, but couldn't find work. We came here November, and there was no work available. And he suffered terrible. Because in the morning when he took me to the hospital to work, he always said, I brought you to the United States that you should work and I can't find work. After a few weeks, an Italian guy in the city hall, [NON-ENGLISH], he had pity on him, and gave him a job as a city worker to clean snow. Because we came in November. So when it started to be cold, but snow didn't fall. So he had a job cleaning snow. But if snow didn't fall, he was out of a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he became so discouraged that one day he boarded a train, and he went to Patterson where his aunt lived. His cousin came running to the hospital, thinking that my husband ran away from me, and took me out from the hospital. And my boss in the hospital loved my work, being that I was so dedicated, tried to persuade me that I should stay there, and because every nurse had their nurse's room, a quarter. So she would give me the key to my room that I should bring my husband. She gave me permission that I should stay already. Because luggage we didn't have. We only had a few belongings that we brought. She says, you could stay as long as you want, till your husband would find a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin said, no. I'm taking you back to Passaic. She put me on a train. And I came at night, I came back to Passaic. And my husband called. And he told me that he found a job in Patterson. An Italian man, because my husband was fluently speaking Italian, the language. So the guy had pity on him, and told him how to work a machine. And he taught him how to work. And he gave him a job for $26 a week. And he came the next morning, picked me up from Passaic. And we went to Patterson. And we found a room, a furnished room by a woman on the next street, a Mrs. Lipschitz. And we went on our own. Because my husband brought $26. It was a fortune for us. We came without a penny. So it was $40 a month. But we couldn't afford to pay $40. So we paid a week $10. And for $5 I went for food shopping. And I became a balabusta on our own in the United States. From there, I found a job in a shop. Because I could not find a job as a nurse, because it required for me to go two years back to school to have my certification in the United States. But because I was penniless, and I couldn't afford to go to school, so I threw myself into sewing. And I went to work in a shop. And we both worked, and we saved a few dollars. And we rented our first three-room apartment. That was our first heaven. In this three-room apartment, I had both of my children. When I held my first child in my hand, it was God's blessing, continuation of life and hope for my future. I saw in my child all my dreams and all my hopes that I ever had during my six years that I was so lost, a lost child under so oppression. But when I was sitting and feeding my child I saw in her my future, my life, my dreams. And we were so happy. My husband and I, we couldn't believe that this apartment is ours that this is our heaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my husband worked, and I started to supplement every penny from the house. I started to sew for customers. I had my second daughter, Arlene, in the same apartment. And she was my second dream come true. I had two beautiful children. What more could I ask of life? An adoring husband that worked and adored us all.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the three-room apartment, we saved our money from hard work. We put a down payment on our first house in Patterson, a two-family house. It was like a castle for us. Because we had a tenant upstairs, so it helped us pay. And it was-- we made it a heaven for us. My husband became very involved. He was the ground breaker from-- [NON-ENGLISH], Zionist Organization. He also played in Patterson with a dramatic group. Because that was his love. He loved it since childhood. And my small children went to see their father playing on the stage. I don't know if my younger daughter remembers, but my older daughter. She has recollection seeing daddy on the stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What more can I want from life? My husband was a wonderful husband, worked very hard. I worked next to him. I build up my business from home, in sewing, fashion designing. We made a living. We lived [NON-ENGLISH]. We dreamt to seeing our children, for their future, a future that my parents dreamt for me and never came to realization. We lived to see both of my children going through schooling, painful it was when they went to school, and I was sitting in the audience when they appeared on the stage. And they had some few lines on the stage, and I was there by myself. And they always came home crying. Why isn't there a grandma or a grandpa together with me? Why do they have no relatives? That I'm always sitting by myself in the audience.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were too young to explain at that time what I went through. The only thing that I could do is give them my love, and to express the sorrow that I had for me and for my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever discuss the Holocaust with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of it, but you couldn't get into details. Because you were too young. See, when they started high school, yes. We started to tell them. It affected them to a certain point. I'm sure that my children were hurt by not having relatives, knowing at that time already that their parents went through such a cruel time in their lives. They finished high school. My older daughter finished college. They got their master's degree. My younger daughter finished college and got her master's degree from Columbia University. My older daughter, Ann, has her master's degree. They're very well-educated children. I'm very proud of them. My husband and I were very proud of our children. Thank heaven we lived to a time that we saw our children get married. My daughter, Ann, married a very fine young man. His name was Neil Metnick. They have two adoring children. I have two beautiful grandchildren that I'm so grateful. My younger daughter got married to Stuart Lehman, a very fine young man, a lawyer. They gave me three beautiful grandsons that I'm so crazy about. I'm crazy about all five grandchildren. They bring me such [NON-ENGLISH], and I'm so proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we close, is there anything that you would like to mention or any experiences during the Holocaust that, you know, I-- I haven't asked you about or that you haven't spoken about?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point I can recollect. I hope that I squeezed six years of my life into the few hours of the interview. I dedicate all my memory and all this interview to the 6 million that didn't survive, to our dear parents that didn't survive, to my dear relatives that didn't come back, and most of all I give it as a testimony to my children and grandchildren that they should remember for just being born a Jew that you could be inflicted so much pain and so much destruction. And I hope and I pray to God that my children and grandchildren that history should never repeat itself. That they should have a good life, that I-- whatever I went through, it should be enough for generations to come. And history may never repeat itself. And I pray for peace in Israel. Because when I went back to Israel, I saw what a beautiful country. And I'm just in love with it. I love Israel. My husband and I, went four times back. And each time we came back we were so proud to be there. And we saw the growth, and the vibration, and the life, and we loved every minute of it. And we are so proud. And even though that I live in the United States, I live with Israel. I am a citizen of the United States, but I am a Jew with Israel. And dear God, the peace should be there. And we should live to see a peaceful generation to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for enduring with me, my pain, and I love you for being with me. I appreciate for the work that you are doing. I think you are all great doing that. Thank you from the bottom of my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my [NON-ENGLISH] from 1946. I was in kibbutz aliyah. And I was honored to be in a group to sail for Israel with the [NON-ENGLISH], to go with a group of Chayalim to go at that time Palestine. Unfortunately it did not materialize. The kibbutz, kibbutz aliyah, I came back because instead of our group, a group of pregnant women sailed for Israel. And I wound up back in kibbutz. This picture of me, I'm Miriam Moskowitz. This picture is of me taken in 1949 to go to the United States. This is made for the papers and for the passport to go to the United States in 1949, made in Italy, Rome. This picture is made in 1945 after I was liberated and I arrived to Italy, and I joined a dramatic group. And this is the picture of my husband, my future husband. At that time, he was director from the Jewish theater. And I joined the group, and I played with him on the stage, entertaining all displaced person camps. This picture was done in 1945 after I was liberated, and arrived to Italy. This is a picture in Italy in 1945, just as I arrived in Italy and I met my future husband. My husband on this picture, he just was liberated from Auschwitz. And he was liberated from the krankenhaus. He came out from the hospital after typhoid fever. He was very skinny, unable to eat. On this picture he is with a friend from his city. His name is [NON-ENGLISH]. He is presently in the United States. On this picture, I am in Italy. I had a few months before, I had my appendix out. And I was very weak. And I was here. That picture is taken in a sanatorium in Rome in a convalescent home. In this picture is with my friend. Her name is [NON-ENGLISH]. At present, she's in Australia. We were very good friends at that time. This picture is taken in Rome in a convalescent home. This picture in front of me is a picture made in Italy when we were in embarkation center, Bagnoli, in Italy. This picture was taken when we were on the way to go to the United States. The embarkation center, they processed a lot of paperwork, and were waiting for our boat to arrive. I am here with my husband on the picture, with some of my friends that they were with me. The picture in front of me shows me and my husband in Salerno. This is the stopover near Naples. This is right before we went on the boat, before we sailed to the United States. It was a happy moment for us, full of hope, full of wishes, for hope to come true. And we were waiting for this moment a long, long time, with hope in our hearts. This is one of my precious possessions, a picture of me and my husband of our precious moments in kibbutz aliyah in Italy. Those are our precious moments of being in love, being young, full of hope for the future, and very much in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945. This picture was taken in 1945 in kibbutz aliyah, in Italy. This is a picture, our first picture and the United States of me and my precious husband, Sol. This picture was taken when we had our-- first have our three-room apartment. And we had so much happiness. In fact, when this picture was taken, the next day I found out that I am pregnant with my first child. And we were exhilarated. We were so happy then. This picture was taken in 1951, in Patterson, New Jersey. This picture in front of me represents another precious moment in my life, when my family and my husband was together with us. From left to right, my granddaughter Stacy is holding in her lap my wonderful grandson Andrew. Above her is my younger daughter, Arlene. Next to her is my daughter Ann. I am next to her. That's me. Next to me as my husband. Below me is my wonderful grandson, David, and my grandson Mathew that it's his birthday that we observe, and the far right is my wonderful grandson, Joel. I love them all. They're all precious to me. And that's the only thing that I have in the whole world is them. This is a very important picture to me. I'm surrounded by five most precious jewels. Those are my five grandchildren, my adored five grandchildren. They're celebrating here in this picture my 69th birthday. Unfortunately, this is a very painful year for me, because that's the year that I lost my husband that I adored, I loved. My grandchildren are here from left to right, Matthew is next to me. Next to me also is Andrew. And the top is David. My only princess, granddaughter Stacy. And below her is my precious Joel. That's Joel. I love you all children. It's too bad that grandpa didn't live to see you grow up and have [NON-ENGLISH] the way I do that he didn't see you going off to college. Stacy is are second-year college now. I wish you good luck, sweetheart. I love you. And David, David I'm so proud of you. You're venturing now. You're on the way to college. And I wish you the best of luck. And Matthew, next year is your bar mitzvah. I hope, in memory of your grandfather that I should be well, and I should be worthy to seeing your bar mitzvah. And Andrew and Joel, I pray to God that I should have healthy years to see you grow up, and have [NON-ENGLISH]. And to cherish grandpa's memory, stay well, children. I love you. The picture in front of me is a testimonial in memory of my dear husband who passed away two years ago on August 9, 1994. I cherish your memory, Sol. I loved you so much. You gave me wonderful years. You were a wonderful father. You were a wonderful husband. You gave me so much happiness. This picture I dedicate to you, in the memory of your dear parents, of your dear brother and sister that perished in the Holocaust. I'm sorry that you are not here to testify in your own words. You were always better than me with words. You endured your young life. You were born in Bialystok in 1923, on December 10th. You went through in your young life, the ghetto in Pruzhany, Bialystok, Auschwitz, Birkenau, and Mauthausen. You lived through hell, just as I did, or maybe more so. The stories that you told me, it was excruciating just to listen that one human being could endure so much pain in his young life. But you were a man with a good heart, and you brought a lot of happiness into my life, into the life of your children. You gave so much love of yourself. May this testimony to you bring memories to your children. Your children loved you so special. You're a special grandfather to your adoring five grandchildren. They think of you. They talk about you. They loved you so much. I do too, Sol. I miss you. May the pain that you endured during the war and the loss of your parents and your family, may they be good [NON-ENGLISH] for all of us. I love you, Sol, And I miss you terrible.
